title: Wine Growth Summit facilitates sharing of international expertise with Aussie industry professionals
summary:
The 2026 Wine Growth Summit hosted by Wine Communicators of Australia was recently held at the Adelaide Convention Centre, with over 15 guest speakers bringing international wine industry insights and strategies to Australia. Speaker Angelica Crabb, senior analyst at Wine Australia, highlighted that Brazil, Mexico, Thailand and India are all expected to import more wine over the next five years. Brendan Carter, winemaker at Unico Zello and director of media company Bottle Shock, spoke of the dynamic and youthful wine culture in Thailand. Andrew Kamphuis, founder and president of Commerce7, offered insights from the e-commerce platforms’ top performing wineries. Emily Harper, chief marketing officer at Australian Vintage, shared lessons from the Poco Vino brand story. Louis Schofield of World Apart Wines encouraged the audience to rethink what growth means to them and chief operating officer for the Randall Wine Group Alexia Roberts stressed the importance of showing up in person and having patience.

The post Wine Growth Summit facilitates sharing of international expertise with Aussie industry professionals appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
By Kyra Trussell

The 2026 Wine Growth Summit hosted by Wine Communicators of Australia was recently held at the Adelaide Convention Centre, with over 15 guest speakers bringing international wine industry insights and strategies to Australia.

Speaker Angelica Crabb, senior analyst at Wine Australia, highlighted that Brazil, Mexico, Thailand and India are all expected to import more wine over the next five years.

Angelica Crabb, senior analyst at Wine Australia

“This increase won’t offset the larger decreases in the bigger markets on a global scale, but for individual businesses willing to diversify, these could be markets worth taking a closer look at,” she said.

Crabb also drew attention to Japan being “second only in white wine consumption to Australia in the Asia Pacific” before detailing other key export countries in the region.

“Looking at Australian exports of white wines to key Asian destinations, you can see that there’s a generally positive trend to destinations like Hong Kong, Thailand, Singapore and South Korea, but what really stands out are the exports to China – they are significantly different now than before the tariffs. 17% of the volume of exports to China is now white wine, and it was 4% in 2020.”

Crabb reassured the audience that it’s not all sour grapes for red wine exports.

“If you exclude China and Hong Kong, Cab Sav exports are up by 30% in value in the last 12 months. Cabernet Sauvignon is growing very well into Canada at the moment, but the next four markets that are growing are Malaysia, Singapore, Thailand and Indonesia. Basically, the Southeast Asian markets are drinking more premium Cabernet Sauvignon from Australia.

“Red is a key wine style in these markets, and it’s a very similar story to Shiraz. For Shiraz, excluding China and Hong Kong, the growth is 7% year on year. The fastest growing market is Thailand, followed by Singapore, Canada and Malaysia,” she shared.

Brendan Carter, winemaker at Unico Zello and director of media company Bottle Shock, spoke of the dynamic and youthful wine culture in Thailand.

Brendan Carter, Unico Zello winemaker and director of Bottle Shock

“The thing that got me was the culture between different venues…the operators help each other; it’s a part of their culture. The attitude is very much ‘if someone gets excited about wine, they’re going to go into my neighbour’s venue, and that is a net good for everyone’. They even host each other’s events to try to act as activations, so they’re very, very close,” he revealed.

Carter also detailed the lack of a gender equality issue in Thailand’s wine industry.

“I went to about 25 wine bars in a week, across all of them, all of them, the importers, the buyers, owners, sommeliers and the people with actual purchasing power, there was simply no gender problem to speak of. Women aren’t really fighting for a seat at the Thai wine table, because they literally built the table.

“Interestingly, when I questioned this, they thought it was the dumbest question I could ask. It’s not framed as an achievement in Thailand, because it’s just not an issue, which is his own kind of remark.”

As Carter frankly put it, Thailand is “just f*cking around and finding out”.

Andrew Kamphuis, founder and president of Commerce7, offered insights from the e-commerce platforms’ top performing wineries.

Andrew Kamphuis, founder and president of Commerce7

“The best wineries in the world, they capture data. The best wineries capture 92% of email addresses at the cellar door…when we look at the average, it’s about 50% (46%).

“Our top 10% of wineries are emailing their entire customer base an average of 10 times a year. The average winery only sends two and a half emails a year. If you’re going to build lifetime customers, if you’re going to attract people, you need to capture an email, and you need to send it,” he said.

Kamphuis also emphasised the importance of personalising the experience online for customers, suggesting winery websites should recommend products on the home page based on previous purchases and information regarding flavour preferences collected at the cellar door. Including a simple ‘welcome back’ message with the customer’s name when they log in can also be a nice touch.

There were many domestic success stories told, with Emily Harper, chief marketing officer at Australian Vintage, sharing lessons from the Poco Vino brand story.

Emily Harper, chief marketing officer at Australian Vintage

Harper said that the first thing the winery did before planning a new product at all was research. The company spoke to over 3000 people globally to find out what challenges people were facing with wine, what the barriers are, tensions and what the brand is “solving for”.

She stressed the importance of looking outside of wine for inspiration, with the packing and retail display designs for Poco Vino being reportedly influenced by the beauty and cosmetic category, coffee brands and the trend of mini chocolates.

The team behind the little wine also thought about the moments where customers would typically reach for RTDs, spirits and seltzers, asking themselves how wine could play a role in those spaces instead.

“We’ve been in market for eight months, we are now ranged in 17 countries and we’re selling something in the region of 500 bottles every hour of this product – it’s genuinely blown our minds,” Harper shared.

“…but the story of this brand is not the vineyard and the terroir and the provenance – it’s actually just how it fits in your lifestyle and that it’s easy, it’s delicious and it’s accessible…even the tasting notes literally just have these three words on the back, there’s no spiel, we stripped everything back and pulled it back to its kind of most raw but fun state.

“We’re not selling a Shiraz…we’re selling the moment that you’re drinking it. We’re selling joy, we’re selling connection, we’re selling that moment where you’re sitting in that meal…that’s what we’re selling. So, bring that to people, and bring that to people in a really engaging way,” she advised.

Bringing an Adelaide local lens and down-to-Earth spirit to the event, Louis Schofield of World Apart Wines encouraged the audience to rethink what growth means to them.

Louis Schofield, founder of World Apart Wines

“Today is about growth, and there’s this great paradox of the industry is not growing, but we keep talking about how we grow. For me, growing is not necessarily about making more money, making more wine, you know, getting more employees, whatever it is…but we do still want to diversify and have some different markets.

“We’ve recently got rid of all of our distributors in Australia, and we now sell the wine ourselves in every market, so I’m doing a lot of domestic travel. I think there’s a great question that keeps coming back to me – why do we need to keep growing?

“These are the things that are important to us in our winery. It isn’t trying to get to 10,000 cases, it isn’t trying to make a million dollars a year turnover – it’s being in the winery with our little kids, it’s being resilient, it’s being sustainable and it’s spending time in my garden. That’s what we love. So, that’s what the growth means to us,” he affirmed.

On the topic of expanding into new markets, chief operating officer for the Randall Wine Group Alexia Roberts stressed the importance of showing up in person and having patience.

Alexia Roberts, chief operating officer at Randall Wine Group

“You know, I think COVID taught us all that we could do stuff by teams, and we all thought, ‘beauty, days of getting on planes are over’, but the reality is that nothing is better than having a conversation in person, face-to-face. Showing up for success is absolutely critical.

“This is an incredibly complex market – some people find it too hard, to be honest, because there are so many nuances in this market – but I promise you, if you take the time and effort to understand it by province, not by country, you will be rewarded,” she said.

Roberts also shared how being flexible can lead to unexpected success, using the launch of the One Pound Per Acre brand as an example.

“Our strategy was about growing premium brands globally, and all of a sudden, we were looking at a $9.95 proposition on the shelf for Chardonnay and Shiraz – but guess what, China was closed, and it seemed like a good opportunity at the time. So, we pivoted. There was an opportunity in front of us, and we pivoted, and we built this brand.

“We launched it in September 2023 with two wines, Chardonnay and Shiraz, and we launched it to fill a gap in the Australian category in Ontario. So [in] one province we launched this wine. 18 months from launch date we were the sixth top Australian wine brand by sales in the LCDO.”

The post Yangarra Estate appoints new winemaking and viticulture leadership team appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Guillaume Camougrand, Shelley Torresan and Cliff Wickman. Images supplied

McLaren Vale’s Yangarra Estate has today announced its new winemaking and viticulture leadership team, appointing Guillaume Camougrand as head of winemaking and viticulture, Shelley Torresan as senior winemaker and Cliff Wickman as vineyard manager.

The appointments follow the passing of Peter Fraser, who served as Yangarra’s head winemaker and general manager from 2000 to November 2025.

Yangarra Estate said that Fraser was instrumental in establishing it as “one of Australia’s great wine estates”, championing southern Rhône varieties like Grenache and “defining the philosophy that continues to guide the estate today”.

Chairman and proprietor of Jackson Family Wines, Barbara Banke, said that Yangarra has always been defined by the people behind it.

“Guillaume, Shelley, and Cliff, together with the entire Yangarra team, embody the dedication, craftsmanship, and philosophy that Peter Fraser helped build over so many years. We are proud of this team and confident they will continue to build on Yangarra’s remarkable reputation in the years ahead,” Banke said.

Camougrand joined Yangarra in 2014 and was promoted to assistant winemaker in 2017, working across the full scope of production alongside Fraser and Torresan. In his new role, the winery shared that he is responsible for the estate’s overall winemaking and viticulture direction.

Guillaume Camougrand

Since joining Yangarra in 2008, the winery said that Torresan has been central to the winemaking team.

Shelley Torresan

“Her deep understanding of the wines, meticulous attention to detail, and unwavering focus on quality have helped ensure the consistency and quality of the Yangarra wine portfolio,” the company said.

Wickman joined Yangarra in 2025, bringing more than a decade of vineyard experience, with a particular interest in Rhône varieties and old bush vines. He oversees stewardship of the estate’s 170 hectares, including approximately 87 hectares under vine.

Cliff Wickman

title: From Cabernet to climate-ready – The Granite Belt’s variety shift
summary:
By Brendan Black Climate change is affecting many agricultural industries, and winemaking, with its crops always at the mercy of the elements, is one of the most at risk. The Granite Belt, nudging the Queensland-New South Wales border, is one wine region that’s been turning its back on more traditional varieties for a long time. […]

The post From Cabernet to climate-ready – The Granite Belt’s variety shift appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
By Brendan Black

Climate change is affecting many agricultural industries, and winemaking, with its crops always at the mercy of the elements, is one of the most at risk. The Granite Belt, nudging the Queensland-New South Wales border, is one wine region that’s been turning its back on more traditional varieties for a long time. Its embrace of grapes that are more suited to these changing climatic conditions has unlocked new opportunities for growth at a very challenging time.

The Granite Belt has faced somewhat of an identity crisis for years: far from major cities like Brisbane and the Gold Coast; believed to be a scorching or subtropical area; and not “serious” when it comes to wine. Yet many people, both consumers and those within the wine industry, have come to discover that there’s quality wine being made in this relatively cool region – much of it from alternative varieties – and it’s worth the travel time.

In fact, the Granite Belt has totally welcomed the non-traditional with ‘Strange Birds’, producing a wine guide that highlights grapes “grown on the Granite Belt [which] represent less than 1% of Australia’s vines” and the 33 wineries that grow them¹. Below are four wineries that have flocked to the Strange Birds.

 

Planting for the future

Mike Hayes is a fourth-generation Granite Belt local and the winemaker at Balancing Heart Vineyard. He’s an extremely knowledgeable and passionate man who’s now entered his 47th year working in the wine industry. In his 40s he undertook a Masters, looking at emerging grape varieties that would be suitable for planting in Australia to withstand climate change.

Hayes researched 650 varieties throughout 50 European wine regions, ultimately setting up a “vineyard of the future” in Queensland with 80 varieties. But there is still a lot of work to be done to convince people of the benefits of these alternate varieties for Australian conditions.

“I did some lectures and was talking about some of the varieties I’d found that I felt they should be looking at,” explained Hayes.

“They still wanted to grow Cabernet, Shiraz, Semillon or Chardonnay and I said, ‘Look, do you realise that the way the climate is altering, you may not be able to grow those varieties?” There are grapes grown on the Iberian Peninsula that can withstand temperatures up to 52°C, but people are still wrapped up in tradition.”

Growing within the Balancing Heart vineyard are some of the usual suspects – Cabernet, Shiraz, Viognier, Chardonnay and Pinot Gris – but also late-ripening varieties such as Mourvèdre, Carmenère, Sagrantino and Refosco del Peduncolo Rosso; there are also 35 rows of Muscat Blanc à Petits Grains that are now over 70 years old. Hayes believes Georgian varieties would also grow well here (there’s already some Saperavi plantedin the region), likewise Greeks such as Assyrtiko and Agiorgitiko, and Cypriots like Maratheftiko and Xynisteri.

A short distance away is Dear Vincent winery, run by Adam Penberthy, who’s also the proprietor of the Ballandean Pub, and was one of the founders of City Winery in Brisbane. He moved to the region looking for a “weekender” but instead bought a vineyard and the pub. Penberthy is “interested in making fun wines that people can drink with mates, rather than with Nana on a Sunday,” although many grandmas seem to love his pet nats.

Most of the vineyard was planted around 25 years ago, with some traditional varieties but also Fiano, Montepulciano, Nebbiolo, Viognier and Verdelho.

“All consumers are starting to embrace alternative varieties,” said Penberthy.

“There are diehards who prefer certain grapes – ‘I only drink Cabernet, I don’t drink Shiraz’ – but most people are intrigued by the alternatives. And the beauty of this region is there’s no history or deep-rooted traditions we need to uphold from a winemaking perspective.”

Despite the embrace of alternative varieties within the region, the subject of climate change leads to much debate.

“If you ask some of the older wine folk in the region, they might say that climate change is really just the normal ups and downs with vintages early, vintages late.Even in my short time here, vintage has gotten earlier and earlier every year, but then if I ask other people, they might say ‘oh, it did that 25 years ago as well’.”

Penberthy’s neighbour to the north is Rokkwell vineyard, purchased by Dawn Walker and partner Jaymie in 2019. Walker then put her Bachelor of Science degree (majoring in Wine Science and Plant Agricultural Science) to good use, becoming assistant winemaker at Ridgemill Estate near Stanthorpe, before moving to full-time at her own winery in 2021.

The vineyard is planted predominantly to Italian varieties: Nebbiolo, Nero d’Avola, Teroldego, Arneis and Garganega, with a small parcel of Sauvignon Blanc. “A lot of people who come to the Granite Belt, they either already know about the ‘Strange Birds’, or they don’t and they learn about them and they find that everywhere they go there might be something different. It’s one of the reasons I was drawn to the Granite Belt myself.”

Walker has also seen climatic changes in the vineyard, with the 2026 vintage being a particularly warm and speedy one.

“Everything grew very quickly,” she detailed.

“I had veraison in mid- December, whereas it would usually be between Christmas and the New Year, so everything was a bit rushed this time, yet we’re definitely still a cool-climate region.”

A few kilometres west of the township of Ballandean is Golden Grove Estate, established in 1946 by Mario and Nita Costanzo as a stone fruit and grape farm. Eventually, the grapes won out and more varieties were planted, first by son Sam, and now the winery is run by grandson Ray and wife Jenny.

Like at Balancing Heart, there are some old rows of Muscat, which was originally grown as a table grape in the area until people realised the liquid gold it could create. The Shiraz vines are 60 years old and the Cabernet is 40, but it’s some of the younger, alternative grapes that are gaining attention. From 2002, other varieties planted include Tempranillo, Barbera, Mourvèdre, Vermentino, Nero d’Avola, Sangiovese and Montepulciano – several of which are medal and trophy winners.

Drought, the last of which ended in 2020, is always around the corner, and with a moratorium on new dams, vines are regularly under pressure.

“The soils drain quickly so you need to keep up the irrigation. Plus, as there are shallow rock shelves, some of the vines, such as Barbera, can still become stressed.”

Since Ray began working at the winery a few decades ago, like winemakers in many other regions, he’s noticed vintages getting shorter.

“We’ve come forward at least a month in picking time,” he said.

“This will be the first Easter that I’ll ever have off as a winemaker.”

 

Planting to site, not fashion

Across the road from Golden Grove is Ballandean Estate, established by Sicilian immigrant Salvatore Cardillo in the 1930s and now the oldest continually running winery in Queensland. In 1968, the table grapes were replaced by Shiraz by Angelo Puglisi, Salvatore’s grandson, who is seen by many as the founder of Queensland’s wine industry. Alongside wife Mary, he later planted Cabernet, Chardonnay, Semillon and Riesling; today, there’s also Silvaner, Fiano, Saperavi, Nebbiolo, Moscato Giallo and Malvasia.

“We used to plant what was popular because it was hard to sell anything else, but now we plant to site – we find out what’s going to suit us best, hence the success of the Saperavi,” said Leeanne Puglisi-Gangemi, Angelo and Mary’s daughter and the client relations manager.

“In the very early days, it was what the customer wanted, but now the weirder it is and as long as it’s grown in the Granite Belt and we call it a Strange Bird, people want to drink it.”

“It’s been an exciting thing to do, and it’s allowed grapegrowers like Dad to make more informed decisions; he can go out there and find these varieties that suit our patch, rather than having to worry about if the customer will actually buy it.”

While there doesn’t seem to be any consensus among the winemakers on the causes of climate change – natural versus anthropogenic – there’s wide agreement that the climate is indeed changing, with both negative and, unexpectedly, positive consequences.

“Some of the traditional varieties are doing better now,” said Puglisi-Gangemi.

“The Cabernet often wouldn’t be ripe until May, yet it’s already picked now [in March]. Dad believes it’s a cycle and more traditional timings will return, but if it is a permanent change, I’m not sure it’s a bad thing.”

Nevertheless, this seems to be one Australian wine region which is preparing itself well against climate change with its embrace of alternative varieties.

As Hayes says, “One good thing about the Granite Belt is that we’ve shown the rest of Australia it can be done.”

 

References

¹ www.granitebeltwinecountry.com.au/strangebird

title: Whitehaven celebrates 93-points from Robert Parker Wine Advocate
summary:
Whitehaven’s Sauvignon Blanc 2025 has been awarded a 93-point rating from Robert Parker Wine Advocate, following a Diamond Trophy at the Sakura Japan Women’s Wine Awards and a Gold Medal at Mundus Vini. The winery said that the score places the Sauvignon Blanc “firmly within the ‘outstanding’ category” on the publication’s scale. Wine critic Erin Larkin highlighted its “intensity and precision” in her review, describing it as “piercing and intense” with citrus, green apple and tropical fruit notes. She noted the wine was saline and pure. Whitehaven Wines co-founder Sue White said that the accolade is a fantastic recognition of the quality and consistency of the flagship wine.

The post Whitehaven celebrates 93-points from Robert Parker Wine Advocate appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Whitehaven’s Sauvignon Blanc has been well-awarded this year. Image supplied

Whitehaven’s Sauvignon Blanc 2025 has been awarded a 93-point rating from Robert Parker Wine Advocate, following a Diamond Trophy at the Sakura Japan Women’s Wine Awards and a Gold Medal at Mundus Vini.

The winery said that the score places the Sauvignon Blanc “firmly within the ‘outstanding’ category” on the publication’s scale.

Wine critic Erin Larkin highlighted its “intensity and precision” in her review, describing it as “piercing and intense” with citrus, green apple and tropical fruit notes. She noted the wine was saline and pure.

Whitehaven Wines co-founder Sue White said that the accolade is a fantastic recognition of the quality and consistency of the flagship wine.

“The 2025 vintage showcases everything we love about Marlborough Sauvignon Blanc – vibrant aromatics, purity of fruit and refreshing acidity – and we are delighted to see it recognised by such a respected international authority,” she said.

Whitehaven produces the “#1 Ultra-Premium Sauvignon Blanc in the USA and the #4 Sauvignon Blanc in the USA across all categories”.

title: Wine festival organisers shortlisted at 2026 New Zealand Event Awards
summary:
Event promoters Ripe Events have been announced as finalists in two categories at the 2026 New Zealand Event Awards. This year, Ripe Events has been recognised as a finalist for the following categories and corresponding events: Best Food, Beverage or Lifestyle Event: Ripe - The Wānaka Wine & Food Festival and Sustainability Initiative of the Year: The Valley Festival. Ripe Events said that the Sustainability Initiative nomination is “particularly meaningful” as the organisation won this same award in 2025 for its work on Ripe Festival, “highlighting the company’s ongoing commitment to creating events that deliver positive environmental and social impact”. The post Wine festival organisers shortlisted at 2026 New Zealand Event Awards appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The company are finalists in two categories and have been shortlisted for a publicly voted award. Image supplied

Event promoters Ripe Events have been announced as finalists in two categories at the 2026 New Zealand Event Awards.

This year, Ripe Events has been recognised as a finalist for the following categories and corresponding events:

  • Best Food, Beverage or Lifestyle Event: Ripe – The Wānaka Wine & Food Festival
  • Sustainability Initiative of the Year: The Valley Festival

Ripe Events said that the Sustainability Initiative nomination is “particularly meaningful” as the organisation won this same award in 2025 for its work on Ripe Festival, “highlighting the company’s ongoing commitment to creating events that deliver positive environmental and social impact”.

Director Nathan White said that the organisation is incredibly proud to be recognised again by the New Zealand Event Association.

“Winning two awards last year was an amazing achievement for our team, and to return this year with two finalist nominations is a reflection of the passion, innovation and commitment that goes into every event we deliver.

“We’re especially proud to see both ‘Ripe – The Wānaka Wine & Food Festival’ and ‘The Valley Festival’ recognised. These events represent the collaborative relationships we’ve built with our local community in mind, and we’re grateful to everyone who has helped bring them to life.”

In addition to these finalist nominations, Ripe Festival has also been shortlisted for New Zealand’s Favourite Event – a publicly voted award celebrating the events that have captured the hearts of New Zealanders.

Ripe Events is calling on attendees, partners, suppliers and supporters to help Ripe Festival take home the title by casting their vote here or on the company’s social pages.

“To be recognised by industry peers is incredibly rewarding, but to be recognised by the people who attend and support our events would be something truly special. We’d love the community to get behind Ripe Festival and help us bring home New Zealand’s Favourite Event,” said White.

The New Zealand Event Awards celebrate excellence across New Zealand’s events industry, recognising organisations and professionals who demonstrate outstanding creativity, innovation and event delivery.

The winners of the 2026 New Zealand Event Awards will be announced later this year.

title: Central Otago’s Young Winemaker and Young Viticulturist of the Year winners announced
summary:
The winners for Central Otago’s Young Winemaker and Young Viticulturist competition were announced yesterday at a combined awards dinner held at Bannockburn Hall. Lisa Fromont from Te Kano won the 2026 Tonnellerie de Mercurey Central Otago Young Winemaker of the Year award whilst Matthew Kerruish from Vinewise Viticulture took home the 2026 Ormond Nurseries Central Otago Young Viticulturist of the Year title. Fromont is primarily based at Te Kano’s Waitaki Estate, where she is 2IC in both the vineyards and the winery. Kerruish works for Vinewise as well as at his family vineyards and winery Folding Hills Wines in Bendigo. At the same award night, Joshua Irving from Lincoln University became the 2026 Tonnellerie de Mercurey North Canterbury Young Winemaker of the Year.

The post Central Otago’s Young Winemaker and Young Viticulturist of the Year winners announced appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The Viticulturist of the Year contestants. Images supplied

The winners for Central Otago’s Young Winemaker and Young Viticulturist competition were announced yesterday at a combined awards dinner held at Bannockburn Hall.

Lisa Fromont from Te Kano won the 2026 Tonnellerie de Mercurey Central Otago Young Winemaker of the Year award whilst Matthew Kerruish from Vinewise Viticulture took home the 2026 Ormond Nurseries Central Otago Young Viticulturist of the Year title.

Fromont is primarily based at Te Kano’s Waitaki Estate, where she is 2IC in both the vineyards and the winery. Kerruish works for Vinewise as well as at his family vineyards and winery Folding Hills Wines in Bendigo.

At the same award night, Joshua Irving from Lincoln University became the 2026 Tonnellerie de Mercurey North Canterbury Young Winemaker of the Year.

Irving also recently became this year’s North Canterbury Young Viticulturist of the Year. Due to winning the title for each competition, he will be the first person ever to compete in both the Young Viticulturist and Young Winemaker national finals in the same year.

“I know there’s a lot of hard work ahead, but I see it as a great opportunity and am excited to seize it,” he said.

Young Winemaker contestants

Congratulations also go to Matt O’Connor from Prophet’s Rock who came third in the Young Winemaker competition.

Rachel Tonk won the Constellation Brands Laboratory Section and Fromont took out the Fruitfed Supplies Best Speech and the Indevin Wine Judging section.

The other contestants were Lachie John, Mount Edward; Maggie Wise, VinPro; Matthew Kerruish, Matt Connell Wines; and Regina Marks, Monte Christo.

Young Viticulturist contestants

The Young Viticulturist competition was held at Otago Polytechnic’s Central Campus on Thursday.

New Zealand Winegrowers congratulated runner ups Braxton Benseman from Mt Difficulty who came second and to Chavi Cleworth from Carrick who came third.

Cleworth also won the BioStart Hortisports whilst Benseman came first in the Syngenta Biologicals Nutrition Section. Jack McMonagle took out the head-to-head Ecotrellis section.

Savannah McGhie from Burn Cottage was awarded the Ormond Nurseries Professional Reputation Award for her “positive, friendly attitude as well as huge growth over the last year”.

The other contestants included Olive Roberts (Peregrine), Abby Crawford and Hamish Anderson – both from Felton Road.

The National Finals

Matthew Kerruish is reportedly thrilled to go through to the national final and is “determined to do Central Otago proud” as he competes for the overall title of 2026 Ormond Nurseries Young Viticulturist of the Year, being held in August.

Lisa Fromont will be on the Burgundy Exchange, however, that experience has a date clash with the national finals, also being held in August. This means she will not be representing Central Otago at the national finals of the 2026 Tonnellerie de Mercurey Young Winemaker of the Year.

The regional chairs were Annabel Bulk and Larissa Woods from Felton Road. Having both competed previously, New Zealand Winegrowers said the two “enjoy giving back and helping the next generation come through”.

content:
The event aims to bring together sommeliers, buyers and operators from around the world. Image supplied

Alepat Taylor will host a dedicated wine and hospitality trade day in Melbourne at Rydges on 27 July.

The event, titled ‘Beyond the Bottle 2026, is designed to “educate, inspire and connect the industry through world-class producers, premium food and cutting-edge hospitality solutions”.

Open exclusively to trade and free to attend, the event will bring together over 35 international and domestic producers. Organisers claim the event presents a “rare opportunity for sommeliers, buyers and operators to engage with the people behind the wines and deepen their understanding of regional styles, philosophy and production”.

Alepat Taylor said that a key focus for this year’s event is “education through exploration”, with a “standout” Italian offering that it believes showcases the country in its entirety and therefore helps attendees build “a comprehensive understanding of one of the world’s most diverse wine landscapes”.

The event will also highlight Australia’s own wine landscape with a selection from South Australia’s Barossa Valley and Clare Valley through to Victoria’s Yarra Valley.

Bruno 1956 (formerly Bruno Fine Foods) will present a range of premium Italian gourmet products while Cedar Hospitality displays a range of catering equipment and service solutions.

Discussion on the future of the Barossa with Sophie Melton of Charles Melton Wines, Adam Fiegert of Gibson and Damien Tscharke of Tscharke Wines

title: California Wines partners with Sydney steakhouse for July-long wine showcase
summary:
California Wines has partnered with Sydney steakhouse The Gidley to present a month-long showcase “celebrating the diversity and quality” of California’s wine regions. The showcase has been designed to introduce Sydney wine lovers to the breadth of styles, appellations and varietals produced across the Golden State. California Wines’ joint regional director for North Asia and Australasia Hiro Tejima said that Australian wine drinkers may only know California for Cabernet Sauvignon expressions – however, he believes that’s only a fraction of what it has to offer. "Australian wine drinkers may know California for certain expressions of Cabernet Sauvignon, but that's only a fraction of what the Golden State has to offer. California is one of the most dynamic wine producing regions in the world, with everything from cool-climate Chardonnay and Pinot Noir to age-worthy reds including Cinsault and Zinfandel from historic old-vine vineyards.”

The post California Wines partners with Sydney steakhouse for July-long wine showcase appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The wines have been curated by Emma Chappell, head sommelier at The Gidley’s. Image credit Dominic Loneragan

California Wines has partnered with Sydney steakhouse The Gidley to present a month-long showcase “celebrating the diversity and quality” of California’s wine regions.

The showcase has been designed to introduce Sydney wine lovers to the breadth of styles, appellations and varietals produced across the Golden State.

California Wines’ joint regional director for North Asia and Australasia Hiro Tejima said that Australian wine drinkers may only know California for Cabernet Sauvignon expressions – however, he believes that’s only a fraction of what it has to offer.

“Australian wine drinkers may know California for certain expressions of Cabernet Sauvignon, but that’s only a fraction of what the Golden State has to offer. California is one of the most dynamic wine producing regions in the world, with everything from cool-climate Chardonnay and Pinot Noir to age-worthy reds including Cinsault and Zinfandel from historic old-vine vineyards.

“As Americans prepare to celebrate Independence Day and the 250th anniversary of the United States, this partnership with The Gidley provides a fitting opportunity for Sydneysiders to discover a wider range of Californian wine through a carefully curated selection of producers and regions,” he said.

Launching on 3 July, the showcase has been curated by The Gidley’s head sommelier Emma Chappel and features 31 Californian wines available by the bottle, with seven new wines not already on The Gidley menu being exclusively available for the duration of the showcase.

When curating the list, Chappell said she wanted to showcase both the wines people expect from California and some they may have never encountered before.

Across the restaurant and lounge, the venue will also offer a California by-the-glass program featuring seven wines, “allowing guests to explore a range of regions and styles without committing to a full bottle”.

“Of course, there are iconic Cabernet Sauvs and Chardonnays, but there are also varieties like Trousseau and Syrah, even sparkling, that demonstrate just how diverse and exciting California has become. The by-the-glass program allows guests to explore multiple regions and styles in a single visit, which is something very few wine lists can offer,” she shared.

The wine menu is subject to demand and will “evolve throughout the month” with rotating selections.

Best known for regions like the Napa Valley and Sonoma County, California produces more than 80% of all American wine and is home to “a diverse collection of coastal, mountain and inland wine regions”.

The restaurant will also offer an “exclusive” Pastrami Beef Rib with Cabernet Glaze and Pickled Cucumber for the duration of the wine promotion.

title: Orange’s Ross Hill Wines releases vintage series, signifying 10 years of carbon neutrality
summary:
The Orange region’s Ross Hill Wines has released a range of new vintage Pinnacle Series wines from winemaker Chris Jessop, marking 10 years of carbon neutral certification. The new wines comprise 2025 Ross Hill ‘Pinnacle Series’ Sauvignon Blanc ($35), 2025 Pinot Gris ($35), 2025 Chardonnay ($40), 2024 Shiraz ($50), 2024 Cabernet Franc ($50) and 2024 Cabernet Sauvignon ($50). Co-owner and vigneron James Robson described the winery’s carbon neutral positioning. “The initial achievement of gaining carbon neutral certification involved a great deal of innovation and effort, but now it is simply how we operate. We continue to innovate in the winery and vineyard, constantly challenging ourselves to lessen our footprint on the land.”

The post Orange’s Ross Hill Wines releases vintage series, signifying 10 years of carbon neutrality appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The winery has been carbon neutral certified for 10 years. Images supplied

The Orange region’s Ross Hill Wines has released a range of new vintage Pinnacle Series wines from winemaker Chris Jessop, marking 10 years of carbon neutral certification.

The new wines comprise 2025 Ross Hill ‘Pinnacle Series’ Sauvignon Blanc ($35), 2025 Pinot Gris ($35), 2025 Chardonnay ($40), 2024 Shiraz ($50), 2024 Cabernet Franc ($50) and 2024 Cabernet Sauvignon ($50).

Co-owner and vigneron James Robson described the winery’s carbon neutral positioning.

“The initial achievement of gaining carbon neutral certification involved a great deal of innovation and effort, but now it is simply how we operate.

“We continue to innovate in the winery and vineyard, constantly challenging ourselves to lessen our footprint on the land.”

Winemaker Jessop said the wines are a product of this philosophy.

“We are using less intervention in our winemaking practices.

“Throughout all the wines in the Pinnacle Series we are using a more supportive oak regime, allowing the pristine, Orange region fruit to step forward. Less new oak, a lighter toast, wild yeast fermentation and a gentler, considered hand has let ‘our place’ be the hero, with fruit purity and texture key drivers in the winemaking decisions,” he explained.

The majority of the grapes for the new Pinnacle Series wines come from Ross Hill’s 13-hectare Griffin Road Vineyard. Planted over 20 years ago, the winery said the vineyard is “well suited to these varieties”, with an elevation spanning 750 to 810 metres.

Jessop has been making wine at Ross Hill for the past two years, having previously made wine at Logan Wines (Mudgee), Grant Burge (Barossa), and Cape Jaffa (Limestone Coast). Jessop has also worked vintages in Germany including at Dr Loosen and, in his homeland, New Zealand.

“The elevation, aspect and soil of our Griffin Road vineyard reliably grow some of the region’s best grapes.

“When many Orange vineyards are marginal with their Cabernet and Cabernet Franc, Griffin Road physiologically ripens these varieties consistently. They are an absolute pleasure to bring into the winery,” said Jessop.

The Ross Hill Range is available nationally via Domaine Wine Shippers. Direct sales and the ‘1994’ Wine Club operate via their cellar door and through the Ross Hill Wines website.
